title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Function -- Liraglutide
content: Liraglutide (Saxenda)  is a daily GLP-1 receptor agonist that pioneered incretin-based obesity therapy (see Table 3 ). Please refer to StatPearls' companion resource, " Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity ," for additional information. While offering more modest weight loss compared to newer weekly agents, liraglutide maintains clinical relevance through its established safety profile, pediatric indication for adolescents 12 years and older, and flexibility for dose adjustments. The SCALE clinical trial program demonstrated a 5.4% to 8.0% weight loss at 56 weeks, with 63.2% of participants achieving a weight loss of 5% or greater.

title: Liraglutide -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Diagnosing and treating type 2 diabetes requires regular collaboration within the healthcare team. Prescribers and pharmacists work together to ensure patients receive their medication at a reasonable cost and within an appropriate time frame. Continuous monitoring of patients' glucose levels assesses the effect of liraglutide on glycemic control. Primary care physicians and advanced-level providers diagnose and treat T2D and manage weight loss. In severe cases where glycemic control is not achieved with first- or second-line treatments, an endocrinologist may be consulted to further evaluate the patient's endocrine dysfunction. For weight loss, patients may require medications such as liraglutide to increase metabolism and reduce appetite. Pharmacists and prescribers communicate with patients to navigate insurance challenges, explore patient assistance programs, and consider alternative payment options to improve access to these medications. Nurses ensure patients understand safe and precise dose administration and drug interactions and educate patients on lifestyle and diet modifications when indicated. Patients taking liraglutide should follow up with their providers every 3 to 6 months to evaluate glycemic control or weight loss progress. Patients with diabetes require close surveillance due to severe complications such as irreversible diabetic neuropathy. Patients taking liraglutide maintain regular appointments and monitor their blood glucose levels. Clinicians, pharmacists, and dieticians work collaboratively to ensure optimal use of liraglutide to treat obesity and T2D.

title: Liraglutide -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
content: For weight loss: The recommended adult dose is 3 mg daily. Patients should start with an initial dose of 0.6 mg daily for the first week and increase the dose by 0.6 mg every week until the target dose of 3 mg is achieved. An outline of the dosing schedule for weight loss is as follows: Week 1: 0.6 mg daily Week 2: 1.2 mg daily Week 3: 1.8 mg daily Week 4: 2.4 mg daily Week 5+: 3 mg daily

title: Special Populations and Long-Term Management in Obesity Medicine -- Clinical Significance -- Evidence-based maintenance strategies
content: Biological factors: Assess metabolic adaptations, including decreased leptin, increased ghrelin, and reduced metabolic rate, which contribute to the regain of weight. Behavioral factors: Evaluate adherence to self-monitoring, physical activity, and meal planning. Identify specific behavioral targets for intervention. Psychological factors: Address discouragement, shame, and "all-or-nothing" thinking patterns that may lead to treatment discontinuation. Treatment intensification: Consider increasing the liraglutide dose, adding combination therapy, or bariatric surgery referral while maintaining behavioral support.

title: Liraglutide -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Liraglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) used to treat type 2 diabetes (T2D) and obesity. This drug belongs to the incretin mimetic class of antidiabetic agents and acts on pancreatic β-cells to stimulate the release of endogenous insulin, helping to control blood glucose levels and suppress appetite. The FDA approved liraglutide in 2010 as a treatment for T2D and in 2014 for weight loss. This activity reviews liraglutide's mechanism of action, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, off-label uses, monitoring, and relevant interactions. These aspects of liraglutide are critical for healthcare professionals managing patients with T2D and related conditions. Understanding liraglutide's proper indications and potential side effects supports effective communication across the interprofessional healthcare team.

title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Clinical Significance -- Liraglutide
content: Contraindications Several other GLP-1 receptor agonists with similar responses in glucose control, weight loss, and cardiovascular benefits have entered the market. However, these medications have not obtained approval for treating overweight or obese patients without diabetes. Researchers have not studied the medication's effects during pregnancy, making it contraindicated in pregnant individuals. It is also not recommended for patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid cancer or MEN 2A or 2B.

title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Numerous clinical trials and studies have been conducted between the short-acting and long-acting GLP-1 analogs to gauge their efficacy, safety, and tolerability. content: Cardiovascular outcomes Several cardiovascular outcome trials have been done on the GLP-1RA. The LEADER-6 trial showed that liraglutide had a cardiovascular benefit in high-risk patients. The SUSTAIN 6 trial demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in death from cardiovascular events with the use of subcutaneous semaglutide; however, it also had the highest decrease in HbA1c, so it was unclear whether the reduction of death from CV events was from the medication or the improved glycemic control. Out of the 7 trials, all have shown noninferiority, and liraglutide, subcutaneous semaglutide, and dulaglutide have significantly reduced cardiovascular outcomes. [20] According to a post hoc analysis of the SUSTAIN and PIONEER trials, semaglutide has shown a decreased relative risk and absolute risk reduction for major adverse cardiovascular events versus its comparators. [21] The AHA emphasizes reducing ASCVD-related outcomes like MI, stroke, and cardiovascular death and recommends GLP-1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide, subcutaneous semaglutide, dulaglutide, and albiglutide, which is currently not available in the US or EU, for their established cardiovascular benefits. [22]

title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Clinical Significance -- Medication Selection
content: Current evidence supports initiating treatment with the most effective medication unless specific contraindications exist. The superior efficacy of tirzepatide and semaglutide compared to other approved medications justifies their use as first-line therapies when clinically appropriate and accessible. [6] [3] Tirzepatide offers the highest efficacy, achieving an average weight loss of 20.2% through its dual mechanism of action, which involves the incretin effect. Semaglutide offers substantial weight reduction, along with additional proven cardiovascular benefits and a well-established safety profile. Alternative agents may be considered when GLP-1 receptor agonists are not feasible or unavailable.

content: In the AWARD-1 trial, dulaglutide was superior to exenatide twice-daily injection. Further trials followed this up, and in the AWARD 6 trial, once-weekly dulaglutide was shown to be as effective as once-weekly liraglutide. Liraglutide, however, had more significant weight loss potential. [16] In another trial, dulaglutide, when compared to liraglutide, had a greater HbA1c reduction. [17] When dulaglutide's initiation in patients was compared to exenatide once weekly and liraglutide, dulaglutide had a higher adherence rate and lower discontinuation rates within 6 months. [18] A more recent randomized controlled trial called SUSTAIN-7 compared semaglutide once-weekly injection to dulaglutide at both low and high doses. Semaglutide showed superiority over dulaglutide in improving glycemic control and weight loss >5%, with both drugs having a similar safety profile. [19]

content: Survodutide (manufactured by Boehringer Ingelheim) is a novel dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist that achieved 18.7% weight loss in phase 2 trials. title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Introduction
content: Obesity has emerged as one of the most significant public health challenges of the 21st century, affecting over 42% of United States adults and contributing to substantial morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. [1] The recognition of obesity as a chronic disease requiring medical management has fundamentally shifted treatment paradigms from short-term interventions to comprehensive, long-term treatment approaches. [2] The therapeutic landscape for obesity has been revolutionized by breakthrough medications that achieve weight loss previously only attainable through bariatric surgery. The introduction of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists and dual incretin receptor agonists has established new standards of care, with some agents producing weight loss of 15% to 25%, compared to 5% to 10% with traditional therapies. [2] [3] Additional benefits, including reductions in cardiovascular risk and improvements in obesity-related comorbidities, have accompanied these advances.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.

content: The pharmacologic management of diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulin and analogue products that replace or augment endogenous insulin, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, biguanides (metformin and fenformin), dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors such as alogliptin and sitagliptin (which inhibit incretin degradation), and glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues such as exenatide, liraglutide and lixisenatide (which mimic GLP‑1 to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delay gastric emptying and suppress appetite). Simvastatin, although a lipid‑lowering statin, is not a DPP‑4 inhibitor. Within the insulin and analogue hierarchy, long‑acting injectable insulins (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec) are formulated alone or combined with a GLP‑1 analogue—insulin glargine/lixisenatide and insulin degludec/liraglutide—providing basal insulin coverage together with the complementary actions of the GLP‑1 component. Another subclass comprises intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins mixed with rapid‑acting insulin to achieve both basal and prandial control; historically, beef insulin, a protein‑based insulin derived from bovine sources, is listed in this combined‑insulin category. This taxonomy illustrates that insulin‑based products directly replace deficient hormone, while non‑insulin agents lower glucose through distinct biochemical pathways, and that combination products pairing long‑acting insulins with GLP‑1 analogues represent contemporary strategies to simplify regimens and achieve synergistic glycemic control.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
content: The community defines “DRUGS USED IN DIABETES” as comprising two major super‑classes: **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES** and **BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS**. Within INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, the subclass **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING** includes agents such as **INSULIN GLARGINE**, the combination product **INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE** (which incorporates **LIRAGLUTIDE**), and **INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE** (which incorporates **LIXISENATIDE**). Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are organized into several pharmacologic classes. **ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS** lower blood glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. **DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 (DPP‑4) INHIBITORS**—including **ALOGLIPTIN** and **SITAGLIPTIN** (the latter also listed as a DPP‑4 inhibitor)—are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. **GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES** comprise **EXENATID**, **LIRAGLUTIDE**, and **LIXISENATIDE**. **BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE LOWER DRUGS** represent another subclass; **FENFORMIN (metformin)** is a member, illustrating that biguanides are a subset of the broader non‑insulin glucose‑lowering category. Combination formulations link agents from different classes, exemplified by **SIMVASTATIN/SITAGLIPTIN**, which pairs the statin **SIMVASTATIN** with the DPP‑4 inhibitor **SITAGLIPTIN**. An important drug‑interaction contraindication is noted: **NELFINAVIR MESYLATE** is contraindicated when used with **SIMVASTATIN**. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies that insulin preparations (including long‑acting analogues) and various oral or injectable non‑insulin agents (alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides) are organized under the overarching category of diabetes medications, with combination products and specific contraindications guiding safe and effective patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
content: The therapeutic category “drugs used in diabetes” comprises insulin‑based products (insulins and analogues) and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Within the insulin family, long‑acting injectable analogues such as insulin glargine, insulin degludec and combination formulations that pair a basal insulin with a rapid‑acting component (e.g., beef insulin and human insulin) are classified as long‑acting or intermediate/long‑acting plus fast‑acting insulins. Fixed‑dose combinations that couple a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—provide complementary glycaemic control. The GLP‑1 analogue class includes exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin, sitagliptin) and biguanides (e.g., fenformin/metformin). Sitagliptin is incorporated in a fixed‑dose tablet with the statin simvastatin; simvastatin is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antiretroviral nelfinavir mesylate. This taxonomy links basal long‑acting insulin analogues, oral biguanides, DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues, highlighting their distinct mechanisms, common combination products, and important drug‑interaction considerations in diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal families: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Insulins and analogues comprise injectable formulations, including long‑acting insulins such as insulin glargine, the combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide; the latter two products incorporate the GLP‑1 analogues liraglutide and lixisenatide, respectively. Blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs excl. insulins are divided into several mechanistic subclasses. Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors lower glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. Dipeptidyl‑peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, a non‑insulin subclass, include alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin / sitagliptin. Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues—exenatide, liraglutide, and lixisenatide—also reduce glucose without containing insulin. Biguanides, another subclass of non‑insulin antihyperglycemic agents, consist of metformin and phenformin (listed as “fenformin”). Together, insulin‑based regimens (both long‑acting and combination products) and the diverse non‑insulin agents—alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides—provide complementary mechanisms to achieve optimal glycemic control in patients with diabetes.
